episode_title: Agnes Stenbom: Reimagining News Through the Eyes of Gen Z

codes.description: Framing news avoidance not as a consumer preference but as a risk to democratic participation, especially for marginalized groups
excerpts: an escalating trend of news avoidance and information fragmentation that really worried me. And we could see certain groups in society not connecting to journalism or with journalism, with in our markets around 30% actively choosing to avoid us

what that will mean in the long term for this group's democratic participation, especially when you look at the fact That news avoiders are typically people who are already marginalized in society in different ways.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Linking news avoidance to social marginalization and low socioeconomic capital rather than mere disinterest
excerpts: If you have a relatively low uh socioeconomic status or capital, that makes you a more likely news avoider, for example.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Young people experiencing anxiety, stress, sadness, and anger from news consumption, framing news as a product with negative emotional effects
excerpts: one of the key pain points that this group experienced was that they feel like consuming the news gives them anxiety. They feel stressed out, they feel sad, angry, often connecting it to issues like racism or prejudice around minority groups.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.code: Responsibility gap between journalists and youth
codes.description: Fundamental disconnect where journalists reject responsibility for emotional impact while youth refuse to consume a product that harms them
excerpts: people within journalism will say that's not our job. We are not responsible for what our journalism makes you feel. While the kids will say, your product makes us feel like shit, so we won't consume it.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Methodology of inviting people disconnected from journalism to participate in designing future news products rather than designing for them
excerpts: we work typically in co-creation processes where we identify groups of We call news outsiders, the people who are on the outside of journalism today. We work together with them to understand current pain points and then uh through speculative design explore what future products, future journalistic products might look like
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Compensating young people from marginalized communities for their time in co-creation processes, treating their input as labor rather than charity
excerpts: we had 10 15-year-olds working with us for 10 weeks. uh coming from a troubled area outside of Stockholm. And they came in through a paid part-time job to work with us in reimagining what news could look like as a product.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Speculative concept of an AI-driven avatar that coaches users through news consumption and helps manage negative emotional responses
excerpts: what they wanted to see Was a feature built into a news product called the News Clinic. And that is a AI-driven news therapist. Essentially self-designed AI avatar that coaches or guides you through news consumption and helps you deal with any potential negative emotion that arises as you consume.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Concept of allowing news consumers to co-create and customize their own AI news delivery personas, raising questions about editorial control
excerpts: could we open up for our users to co-create what our anchors or what our germ lists look like.
you could yourself decide who gives you the news and what that I'm holding up brackets, but what that person looks like, sounds like, feels like
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Concept of an individually adapted AI 'news butler' delivering personalized news in terms of timing, tone, and format
excerpts: an individually adapted news butler that gives you the news when you want it, when, when it might fit you, in a tone of voice that fits you.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Recognition that AI personalization of delivery means news organizations control raw information but not how it reaches audiences
excerpts: That is essentially as this media organizations giving up control of the message, right? What we would control in a future like that is the raw information, but not the way that that information in turn reaches audiences. That I'm not as sure of if that's a good thing or not
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Younger generations have higher expectations for participation and voice in shaping products they consume, unlike older generations
excerpts: They are used to their voice mattering. in a way that older generations have not been taught. So they have much higher expectations on us and our products to allow them to be part of shaping. Shaping the experience.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Prototype converting written articles into AI-generated rap songs, drawing on the human tradition of storytelling through music
excerpts: in a product experiment, we used generative AI to convert classical written articles into AI-generated rap songs.

I'm a human being and human beings have always told stories through music. We tell our or we share kind of educational songs with our babies and we sit by the fire and tell cultural stories through music. So why couldn't we tell news stories through music?
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Personal testimony that music-format news is more memorable than traditional audio formats
excerpts: I remember word by word every every article that we did last year. because it was music and I remember music, but I couldn't tell you what I listened to on my lunch podcast today.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Legal and copyright issues with generative AI training data preventing a successful prototype from going live
excerpts: it's not live right now, largely due to a lot of legal issues with generative AI and who holds the copyright to the original music that led to the training of the generative model, etc. Lots of big questions to deal with.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Young users responding positively not just to the product but to the brand's willingness to experiment
excerpts: Very importantly, that they really found it cool that a traditional newsbrand dared to do something like that
user feedback with kids saying, cool that you're testing this out. Cool that you seem to want to reach us.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Reframing journalistic value as raw information that can be delivered in multiple formats rather than as the article itself
excerpts: What if The product that we're giving isn't the article per se, but the information that could be brought to users in lots of different ways in the version, in the format that they enjoy.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Schibsted's institutional history of uncomfortable-but-successful bets creating organizational capacity for risk-taking
excerpts: Schipstead is a great and often luxurious place to be working with AI in media because we have such a strong legacy. of innovating. We have a legacy of doing things that were very uncomfortable at the time, but turned out to be great bets in the future. So we have kind of a muscle memory that allows us to be a bit risky
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Investing in both technical opportunity training and open conversations about risks and concerns simultaneously
excerpts: We've also made sure to make this topic not just one that is about all the opportunities and all the great things that we should be doing, but also have very public conversations about the risks, about the things that people might be concerned about, that we bring those topics up to the surface so that we don't just kind of suppress that
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Company-wide AI training program with role-specific tracks enrolling thousands of employees across departments
excerpts: We have a program called the Shift AI Academy that has enrolled thousands of employees where anyone from product, business, editorial engineering can engage in a track, a specific track of learning designed for them.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Reframing job displacement debate around preserving journalism's democratic function rather than specific roles or staffing levels
excerpts: we have to lift or we have a responsibility to lift that discussion one or a few levels up. and discuss whether the important thing to protect here or evolve are the individual companies or organizations or number of staffers right now. or the function of journalism and the role that we play in democracies. A certain number of staffers being needed to uphold that function, that's not bound by some natural law.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Vision of future journalism combining human strengths (empathy, creativity) with machine capabilities in paired workflows
excerpts: I find it likely that we'll see much more and many more hybrid workflows. So workflows that pair human excellence and mission functionality.
My research so far identifies things like empathy, creativity, that we are relatively better than the machines. at doing that.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Argument that journalism's future should be shaped not only by journalists but also by civic actors committed to democratic institutions
excerpts: Daring to open up the discussion about journalism to other than journalists could be a starting point.
we invite people who are in other ways committed to sustainable development or democracy issues. So typically people who are involved in civic youth organizations
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Admission that no publisher has a structured answer for accountability when content is produced through opaque generative AI systems
excerpts: we are publishing materials created through ChatGPT, for example. But I don't think that we or any other publisher right now has a good answer related to accountability in that process.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Argument that understanding how AI tools work is not just a technical concern but a prerequisite for editorial accountability
excerpts: I think a lot about how technical oversight is essentially editorial oversight and at the end of the day, accountability. So is it Ethical for us to publish things that are produced in ways that we don't understand if we don't have technical oversight
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Warning against superficial AI experimentation that treats tools as black boxes without curiosity about underlying mechanisms
excerpts: I think it's very important that when we do so, we make sure that we're not just scratching the surface and allowing the big black box to just be a big black box, but that we are curious around more than the very superficial level.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Structured framework (Fairness, Accountability, Sustainability, Transparency) with self-assessment questions for AI product teams
excerpts: FAST is an acronym for fairness, accountability, sustainability, and transparency. And it's a risk assessment framework building on awareness around what the risks in those particular areas are and how you as a product development team can meet those risks.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Local, time-limited AI teams where journalists and designers step out of regular roles to work full-time on AI transformation
excerpts: many of the newsrooms have dedicated AI teams. uh calling them AI hubs or AI task force, etc.
they've had a year of the AI hub working full-time, stepping out of their other tasks or jobs to go full-time in the AI task or the hub to just start transforming the newsroom.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.code: Buddy reader as editorial AI tool
codes.description: Custom GPT providing AI-generated feedback on journalist writing, replacing or supplementing peer review
excerpts: we've developed buddy readers, so tools where journalists instead of giving feedback on each other's text, they could get feedback from a custom GDT.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.code: Anchor in user needs not tech
codes.description: Strategy of pitching AI innovations by starting with audience problems rather than technology capabilities
excerpts: we try to always anchor it in user needs. Not saying you should use this technology because it's new and cool and shiny, but saying here's an audience group that you say that you really want to reach. Here's a problem that they're experiencing, and here's a way that this particular tool can help us solve or meet that problem.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Argument that newsroom culture and organizational transformation ability are the real competitive advantage, not tools, which is why sharing AI knowledge is not threatening
excerpts: the real competitive advantage is our newsroom culture, our organizational ability to transform, to rethink, to innovate. And we can't copy that simply by looking or listening at someone else's presentation. That's work that happens in-house. That's not something you can steal from a network.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Personal choice to use AI intensively at work but rarely in private life, disconnecting efficiency values from personal life
excerpts: I lead a very low tech life. I personally or in my private life, I very rarely use AI when not at the office. So for me, AI is deeply connected to efficiency right now. Creativity also, but but efficiency at work. And when I'm at home in my personal life Efficiency is not a value that is very important to me.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Using speculative prototypes not as products to ship but as provocations that force internal organizational conversations
excerpts: I think a speculative scenario like this Which builds on very real technical capabilities. We could build this if we wanted to. I think that is important to bring to the newsroom to start talking about How we deal with that discrepancy
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: The power of concrete proof-of-concept demos over abstract pitches in overcoming organizational skepticism
excerpts: the initial response was always a little bit of almost laughter, like, huh, that's a fun idea, but yeah. But when I brought the first proof of concept showing this is what the technology can actually do, this is how good it is... They were also really positive. They were stunned by how good it could get.
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Cross-organizational professional community of 450+ members sharing AI journalism practices openly, driven by shared commitment to journalism's democratic function
excerpts: Now that group has grown to, I think we're 450, something like that, in our network now, with people who have either explicit AI journalism roles or who work with AI as part of their product or editorial or strategy roles
we all want that institution to thrive. So if we can help each other in that work, people seem really eager to share
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

codes.description: Doctoral research specifically investigating how human and machine capabilities combine in journalism workflows
excerpts: I'm also an industrial PhD candidate. So I'm writing a doctoral dissertation on AI and journalism and I have been for the past four years.

When it comes to the process, I find it likely that we'll see much more and many more hybrid workflows. So workflows that pair human excellence and mission functionality. This is also what my own research is about
codes.speaker: Agnes Stenbom

episode_summary: Agnes Stenbom articulates a radically audience-centered vision of AI in journalism, rooted in the provocation that 30% of Nordic audiences actively avoid news and that avoidance correlates with social marginalization -- making it a democratic crisis, not merely a business problem. Her most distinctive methodological contribution is co-creation with 'news outsiders,' particularly paid youth from underrepresented communities, which has produced genuinely surprising concepts like an AI news therapist and news-as-rap-songs that challenge journalism's assumptions about format, responsibility, and control. The conversation surfaces a sharp tension between journalist self-understanding (we are not responsible for how our product makes you feel) and youth expectation (your product makes us feel like shit, so we won't consume it), with AI avatars and personalized delivery raising unsettled questions about whether news organizations should cede control of the message while retaining control of information. Stenbom is notably candid about accountability gaps, admitting that Schibsted publishes ChatGPT-generated content without a structured accountability framework, while simultaneously arguing that technical oversight is indistinguishable from editorial oversight.
surprises: Stenbom openly admits Schibsted publishes ChatGPT-generated material without a structured accountability framework, a rare candid admission from a major publisher

The 'News is Music' prototype was halted not for quality or audience reasons but for copyright/legal issues with generative AI training data, suggesting legal frameworks are the binding constraint on format innovation

15-year-olds from marginalized Stockholm suburbs produced the most conceptually provocative AI ideas (news therapist, rap news), suggesting that newsroom innovation may require voices from outside the industry
Stenbom personally leads a 'very low tech' private life despite being deeply embedded in AI work professionally, suggesting a conscious boundary between efficiency-oriented work and personal values

The Nordic AI Journalism Network's open sharing culture contradicts the traditional competitiveness of news organizations, explained by the insight that tools are irrelevant -- organizational culture is the real competitive moat
tensions: Tension between journalism's self-concept as serving the public and its resistance to letting the public participate in shaping what journalism should be
Tension between the 'news therapist' concept (journalism should help manage emotional harm) and traditional journalistic detachment (we are not responsible for how you feel)
Tension between user-designed AI avatars enabling engagement and the risk of news organizations losing control of how information is framed and delivered
Tension between publishing generative AI content in production and the admitted absence of a structured accountability framework for that content
Tension between protecting journalism's democratic function (which may require fewer journalists) and protecting current journalism jobs and roles
Tension between Schibsted's 'innovation muscle memory' narrative and the acknowledgment that employees are still skeptical, scared, or hesitant about AI
